record.example: n=1: z_1 any point in unit disk. |eps_1 z_1| = |z_1| <= 1 <= sqrt(2). Trivial.
n=2, z_1 = z_2 = 1 (real): Choose eps_1 = 1, eps_2 = -1 => sum = 0 <= sqrt(2). Both +1 gives 2 > sqrt(2).
record.why_relevant: Shows importance of sign choice for cancellation
record.where_assumptions_take_effect: Both in unit disk, cancellation possible
record.observed_pattern: Opposite signs can cancel equal vectors

record.example: n=2, z_1 = 1 (real), z_2 = i (imaginary): Choose eps_1 = eps_2 = 1 => |1+i| = sqrt(2). Tight!
record.why_relevant: Shows sqrt(2) is the optimal constant
record.observed_pattern: Bound is tight for perpendicular unit vectors

record.example: n=2, z_1 = sqrt(2), z_2 = i: This violates assumptions since |z_1| = sqrt(2) > 1. Not relevant.
record.why_relevant: Negative example showing why greedy might not work with S = sqrt(2) and z = i
record.observed_pattern: S = sqrt(2) and z = i is not allowed since |z| would need to be 1

record.example: n=3: z_1 = 1, z_2 = e^{i*2pi/3}, z_3 = e^{i*4pi/3}. Choose all eps = 1 => sum = 0 <= sqrt(2). Symmetric configuration cancels naturally.
record.observed_pattern: Equally spaced angles can produce zero sum

record.example: n=3, z_1 = 1, z_2 = 1, z_3 = 1. Choose eps_1 = 1, eps_2 = -1, eps_3 = 0 (not allowed)... Need all signs in {1,-1}. Choose (1,1,-1) => |1+1-1| = 1 <= sqrt(2).
record.why_relevant: Simple cancellation pattern works for many equal numbers
record.where_assumptions_take_effect: Repeated values can be paired to cancel
record.observed_pattern: Pairing of similar vectors produces cancellation
